lines: Now is the winter of our discontent
Made glorious summer by this son of York,
And all the clouds that loured upon our house
In the deep bosom of the ocean buried.
Now are our brows bound with victorious wreaths,
Our bruised arms hung up for monuments,
Our stern alarums changed to merry meetings,
Our dreadful marches to delightful measures.
Grim-visaged war hath smoothed his wrinkled front;
And now, instead of mounting barbed steeds
To fright the souls of fearful adversaries,
He capers nimbly in a lady's chamber
To the lascivious pleasing of a lute.
But I, that am not shaped for sportive tricks,
Nor made to court an amorous looking glass;
I, that am rudely stamped and want love's majesty
To strut before a wanton ambling nymph;
I, that am curtailed of this fair proportion,
Cheated of feature by dissembling nature,
Deformed, unfinished, sent before my time
Into this breathing world scarce half made up,
And that so lamely and unfashionable
That dogs bark at me as I halt by them--
Why, I, in this weak piping time of peace,
Have no delight to pass away the time,
Unless to see my shadow in the sun
And descant on mine own deformity.
And therefore, since I cannot prove a lover
To entertain these fair well-spoken days,
I am determined to prove a villain
And hate the idle pleasures of these days.
Plots have I laid, inductions dangerous,
By drunken prophecies, libels, and dreams,
To set my brother Clarence and the King
In deadly hate, the one against the other;
And if King Edward be as true and just
As I am subtle, false, and treacherous,
This day should Clarence closely be mewed up
About a prophecy which says that "G"
Of Edward's heirs the murderer shall be.
Dive, thoughts, down to my soul. Here Clarence
comes.
fullText: Now is the winter of our discontent Made glorious summer by this son of York, And all the clouds that loured upon our house In the deep bosom of the ocean buried. Now are our brows bound with victorious wreaths, Our bruised arms hung up for monuments, Our stern alarums changed to merry meetings, Our dreadful marches to delightful measures. Grim-visaged war hath smoothed his wrinkled front; And now, instead of mounting barbed steeds To fright the souls of fearful adversaries, He capers nimbly in a lady's chamber To the lascivious pleasing of a lute. But I, that am not shaped for sportive tricks, Nor made to court an amorous looking glass; I, that am rudely stamped and want love's majesty To strut before a wanton ambling nymph; I, that am curtailed of this fair proportion, Cheated of feature by dissembling nature, Deformed, unfinished, sent before my time Into this breathing world scarce half made up, And that so lamely and unfashionable That dogs bark at me as I halt by them-- Why, I, in this weak piping time of peace, Have no delight to pass away the time, Unless to see my shadow in the sun And descant on mine own deformity. And therefore, since I cannot prove a lover To entertain these fair well-spoken days, I am determined to prove a villain And hate the idle pleasures of these days. Plots have I laid, inductions dangerous, By drunken prophecies, libels, and dreams, To set my brother Clarence and the King In deadly hate, the one against the other; And if King Edward be as true and just As I am subtle, false, and treacherous, This day should Clarence closely be mewed up About a prophecy which says that "G" Of Edward's heirs the murderer shall be. Dive, thoughts, down to my soul. Here Clarence comes.

lines: Upon what cause?
fullText: Upon what cause?

lines: Alack, my lord, that fault is none of yours.
He should, for that, commit your godfathers.
O, belike his Majesty hath some intent
That you should be new christened in the Tower.
But what's the matter, Clarence? May I know?
fullText: Alack, my lord, that fault is none of yours. He should, for that, commit your godfathers. O, belike his Majesty hath some intent That you should be new christened in the Tower. But what's the matter, Clarence? May I know?

lines: Why, this it is when men are ruled by women.
'Tis not the King that sends you to the Tower.
My Lady Grey his wife, Clarence, 'tis she
That tempers him to this extremity.
Was it not she and that good man of worship,
Anthony Woodeville, her brother there,
That made him send Lord Hastings to the Tower,
From whence this present day he is delivered?
We are not safe, Clarence; we are not safe.
fullText: Why, this it is when men are ruled by women. 'Tis not the King that sends you to the Tower. My Lady Grey his wife, Clarence, 'tis she That tempers him to this extremity. Was it not she and that good man of worship, Anthony Woodeville, her brother there, That made him send Lord Hastings to the Tower, From whence this present day he is delivered? We are not safe, Clarence; we are not safe.

lines: Humbly complaining to her Deity
Got my Lord Chamberlain his liberty.
I'll tell you what: I think it is our way,
If we will keep in favor with the King,
To be her men and wear her livery.
The jealous o'erworn widow and herself,
Since that our brother dubbed them gentlewomen,
Are mighty gossips in our monarchy.
fullText: Humbly complaining to her Deity Got my Lord Chamberlain his liberty. I'll tell you what: I think it is our way, If we will keep in favor with the King, To be her men and wear her livery. The jealous o'erworn widow and herself, Since that our brother dubbed them gentlewomen, Are mighty gossips in our monarchy.

lines: Even so. An please your Worship, Brakenbury,
You may partake of anything we say.
We speak no treason, man. We say the King
Is wise and virtuous, and his noble queen
Well struck in years, fair, and not jealous.
We say that Shore's wife hath a pretty foot,
A cherry lip, a bonny eye, a passing pleasing tongue,
And that the Queen's kindred are made gentlefolks.
How say you, sir? Can you deny all this?
fullText: Even so. An please your Worship, Brakenbury, You may partake of anything we say. We speak no treason, man. We say the King Is wise and virtuous, and his noble queen Well struck in years, fair, and not jealous. We say that Shore's wife hath a pretty foot, A cherry lip, a bonny eye, a passing pleasing tongue, And that the Queen's kindred are made gentlefolks. How say you, sir? Can you deny all this?

lines: Naught to do with Mistress Shore? I tell thee,
fellow,
He that doth naught with her, excepting one,
Were best to do it secretly, alone.
fullText: Naught to do with Mistress Shore? I tell thee, fellow, He that doth naught with her, excepting one, Were best to do it secretly, alone.

lines: We are the Queen's abjects and must obey.--
Brother, farewell. I will unto the King,
And whatsoe'er you will employ me in,
Were it to call King Edward's widow "sister,"
I will perform it to enfranchise you.
Meantime, this deep disgrace in brotherhood
Touches me deeper than you can imagine.
fullText: We are the Queen's abjects and must obey.-- Brother, farewell. I will unto the King, And whatsoe'er you will employ me in, Were it to call King Edward's widow "sister," I will perform it to enfranchise you. Meantime, this deep disgrace in brotherhood Touches me deeper than you can imagine.

lines: Well, your imprisonment shall not be long.
I will deliver you or else lie for you.
Meantime, have patience.
fullText: Well, your imprisonment shall not be long. I will deliver you or else lie for you. Meantime, have patience.

lines: Go tread the path that thou shalt ne'er return.
Simple, plain Clarence, I do love thee so
That I will shortly send thy soul to heaven,
If heaven will take the present at our hands.
But who comes here? The new-delivered Hastings?
fullText: Go tread the path that thou shalt ne'er return. Simple, plain Clarence, I do love thee so That I will shortly send thy soul to heaven, If heaven will take the present at our hands. But who comes here? The new-delivered Hastings?

lines: As much unto my good Lord Chamberlain.
Well are you welcome to the open air.
How hath your Lordship brooked imprisonment?
fullText: As much unto my good Lord Chamberlain. Well are you welcome to the open air. How hath your Lordship brooked imprisonment?

lines: No doubt, no doubt; and so shall Clarence too,
For they that were your enemies are his
And have prevailed as much on him as you.
fullText: No doubt, no doubt; and so shall Clarence too, For they that were your enemies are his And have prevailed as much on him as you.

lines: What news abroad?
fullText: What news abroad?

lines: Now, by Saint John, that news is bad indeed.
O, he hath kept an evil diet long,
And overmuch consumed his royal person.
'Tis very grievous to be thought upon.
Where is he, in his bed?
fullText: Now, by Saint John, that news is bad indeed. O, he hath kept an evil diet long, And overmuch consumed his royal person. 'Tis very grievous to be thought upon. Where is he, in his bed?

lines: Go you before, and I will follow you.
He cannot live, I hope, and must not die
Till George be packed with post-horse up to heaven.
I'll in to urge his hatred more to Clarence
With lies well steeled with weighty arguments,
And, if I fail not in my deep intent,
Clarence hath not another day to live;
Which done, God take King Edward to His mercy,
And leave the world for me to bustle in.
For then I'll marry Warwick's youngest daughter.
What though I killed her husband and her father?
The readiest way to make the wench amends
Is to become her husband and her father;
The which will I, not all so much for love
As for another secret close intent
By marrying her which I must reach unto.
But yet I run before my horse to market.
Clarence still breathes; Edward still lives and reigns.
When they are gone, then must I count my gains.
fullText: Go you before, and I will follow you. He cannot live, I hope, and must not die Till George be packed with post-horse up to heaven. I'll in to urge his hatred more to Clarence With lies well steeled with weighty arguments, And, if I fail not in my deep intent, Clarence hath not another day to live; Which done, God take King Edward to His mercy, And leave the world for me to bustle in. For then I'll marry Warwick's youngest daughter. What though I killed her husband and her father? The readiest way to make the wench amends Is to become her husband and her father; The which will I, not all so much for love As for another secret close intent By marrying her which I must reach unto. But yet I run before my horse to market. Clarence still breathes; Edward still lives and reigns. When they are gone, then must I count my gains.

lines: Stay, you that bear the corse, and set it down.
fullText: Stay, you that bear the corse, and set it down.

lines: Villains, set down the corse or, by Saint Paul,
I'll make a corse of him that disobeys.
fullText: Villains, set down the corse or, by Saint Paul, I'll make a corse of him that disobeys.

lines: Unmannered dog, stand thou when I command!--
Advance thy halberd higher than my breast,
Or by Saint Paul I'll strike thee to my foot
And spurn upon thee, beggar, for thy boldness.
fullText: Unmannered dog, stand thou when I command!-- Advance thy halberd higher than my breast, Or by Saint Paul I'll strike thee to my foot And spurn upon thee, beggar, for thy boldness.

lines: Sweet saint, for charity, be not so curst.
fullText: Sweet saint, for charity, be not so curst.

lines: Lady, you know no rules of charity,
Which renders good for bad, blessings for curses.
fullText: Lady, you know no rules of charity, Which renders good for bad, blessings for curses.

lines: But I know none, and therefore am no beast.
fullText: But I know none, and therefore am no beast.

lines: More wonderful, when angels are so angry.
Vouchsafe, divine perfection of a woman,
Of these supposed crimes to give me leave
By circumstance but to acquit myself.
fullText: More wonderful, when angels are so angry. Vouchsafe, divine perfection of a woman, Of these supposed crimes to give me leave By circumstance but to acquit myself.

lines: Fairer than tongue can name thee, let me have
Some patient leisure to excuse myself.
fullText: Fairer than tongue can name thee, let me have Some patient leisure to excuse myself.

lines: By such despair I should accuse myself.
fullText: By such despair I should accuse myself.

lines: Say that I slew them not.
fullText: Say that I slew them not.

lines: I did not kill your husband.
fullText: I did not kill your husband.

lines: Nay, he is dead, and slain by Edward's hands.
fullText: Nay, he is dead, and slain by Edward's hands.

lines: I was provoked by her sland'rous tongue,
That laid their guilt upon my guiltless shoulders.
fullText: I was provoked by her sland'rous tongue, That laid their guilt upon my guiltless shoulders.

lines: I grant you.
fullText: I grant you.

lines: The better for the King of heaven that hath him.
fullText: The better for the King of heaven that hath him.

lines: Let him thank me, that holp to send him thither,
For he was fitter for that place than Earth.
fullText: Let him thank me, that holp to send him thither, For he was fitter for that place than Earth.

lines: Yes, one place else, if you will hear me name it.
fullText: Yes, one place else, if you will hear me name it.

lines: Your bedchamber.
fullText: Your bedchamber.

lines: So will it, madam, till I lie with you.
fullText: So will it, madam, till I lie with you.

lines: I know so. But, gentle Lady Anne,
To leave this keen encounter of our wits
And fall something into a slower method:
Is not the causer of the timeless deaths
Of these Plantagenets, Henry and Edward,
As blameful as the executioner?
fullText: I know so. But, gentle Lady Anne, To leave this keen encounter of our wits And fall something into a slower method: Is not the causer of the timeless deaths Of these Plantagenets, Henry and Edward, As blameful as the executioner?

lines: Your beauty was the cause of that effect--
Your beauty, that did haunt me in my sleep
To undertake the death of all the world,
So I might live one hour in your sweet bosom.
fullText: Your beauty was the cause of that effect-- Your beauty, that did haunt me in my sleep To undertake the death of all the world, So I might live one hour in your sweet bosom.

lines: These eyes could not endure that beauty's wrack.
You should not blemish it, if I stood by.
As all the world is cheered by the sun,
So I by that. It is my day, my life.
fullText: These eyes could not endure that beauty's wrack. You should not blemish it, if I stood by. As all the world is cheered by the sun, So I by that. It is my day, my life.

lines: Curse not thyself, fair creature; thou art both.
fullText: Curse not thyself, fair creature; thou art both.

lines: It is a quarrel most unnatural
To be revenged on him that loveth thee.
fullText: It is a quarrel most unnatural To be revenged on him that loveth thee.

lines: He that bereft thee, lady, of thy husband
Did it to help thee to a better husband.
fullText: He that bereft thee, lady, of thy husband Did it to help thee to a better husband.

lines: He lives that loves thee better than he could.
fullText: He lives that loves thee better than he could.

lines: Plantagenet.
fullText: Plantagenet.

lines: The selfsame name, but one of better nature.
fullText: The selfsame name, but one of better nature.

lines: Here. Why dost
thou spit at me?
fullText: Here. Why dost thou spit at me?

lines: Never came poison from so sweet a place.
fullText: Never came poison from so sweet a place.

lines: Thine eyes, sweet lady, have infected mine.
fullText: Thine eyes, sweet lady, have infected mine.

lines: I would they were, that I might die at once,
For now they kill me with a living death.
Those eyes of thine from mine have drawn salt
tears,
Shamed their aspects with store of childish drops.
These eyes, which never shed remorseful tear--
No, when my father York and Edward wept
To hear the piteous moan that Rutland made
When black-faced Clifford shook his sword at him;
Nor when thy warlike father, like a child,
Told the sad story of my father's death
And twenty times made pause to sob and weep,
That all the standers-by had wet their cheeks
Like trees bedashed with rain--in that sad time,
My manly eyes did scorn an humble tear;
And what these sorrows could not thence exhale
Thy beauty hath, and made them blind with
weeping.
I never sued to friend nor enemy;
My tongue could never learn sweet smoothing word.
But now thy beauty is proposed my fee,
My proud heart sues and prompts my tongue to
speak.
Teach not thy lip such scorn, for it was made
For kissing, lady, not for such contempt.
If thy revengeful heart cannot forgive,
Lo, here I lend thee this sharp-pointed sword,
Which if thou please to hide in this true breast
And let the soul forth that adoreth thee,
I lay it naked to the deadly stroke
And humbly beg the death upon my knee.
she offers at it with his sword.]
Nay, do not pause, for I did kill King Henry--
But 'twas thy beauty that provoked me.
Nay, now dispatch; 'twas I that stabbed young
Edward--
But 'twas thy heavenly face that set me on.
Take up the sword again, or take up me.
fullText: I would they were, that I might die at once, For now they kill me with a living death. If thy revengeful heart cannot forgive, Lo, here I lend thee this sharp-pointed sword, Which if thou please to hide in this true breast And let the soul forth that adoreth thee, I lay it naked to the deadly stroke And humbly beg the death upon my knee. she offers at it with his sword.] Nay, do not pause, for I did kill King Henry-- But 'twas thy beauty that provoked me. Nay, now dispatch; 'twas I that stabbed young Edward-- But 'twas thy heavenly face that set me on. Take up the sword again, or take up me.

lines: Then bid me kill myself, and I will do it.
fullText: Then bid me kill myself, and I will do it.

lines: That was in thy rage.
Speak it again and, even with the word,
This hand, which for thy love did kill thy love,
Shall for thy love kill a far truer love.
To both their deaths shalt thou be accessory.
fullText: That was in thy rage. Speak it again and, even with the word, This hand, which for thy love did kill thy love, Shall for thy love kill a far truer love. To both their deaths shalt thou be accessory.

lines: 'Tis figured in my tongue.
fullText: 'Tis figured in my tongue.

lines: Then never was man true.
fullText: Then never was man true.

lines: Say then my peace is made.
fullText: Say then my peace is made.

lines: But shall I live in hope?
fullText: But shall I live in hope?

lines: Vouchsafe to wear this ring.
fullText: Vouchsafe to wear this ring.

lines: Look how my ring encompasseth thy finger;
Even so thy breast encloseth my poor heart.
Wear both of them, for both of them are thine.
And if thy poor devoted servant may
But beg one favor at thy gracious hand,
Thou dost confirm his happiness forever.
fullText: Look how my ring encompasseth thy finger; Even so thy breast encloseth my poor heart. Wear both of them, for both of them are thine. And if thy poor devoted servant may But beg one favor at thy gracious hand, Thou dost confirm his happiness forever.

lines: That it may please you leave these sad designs
To him that hath most cause to be a mourner,
And presently repair to Crosby House,
Where, after I have solemnly interred
At Chertsey monast'ry this noble king
And wet his grave with my repentant tears,
I will with all expedient duty see you.
For divers unknown reasons, I beseech you,
Grant me this boon.
fullText: That it may please you leave these sad designs To him that hath most cause to be a mourner, And presently repair to Crosby House, Where, after I have solemnly interred At Chertsey monast'ry this noble king And wet his grave with my repentant tears, I will with all expedient duty see you. For divers unknown reasons, I beseech you, Grant me this boon.

lines: Bid me farewell.
fullText: Bid me farewell.

lines: No, to Whitefriars. There attend my coming.
Was ever woman in this humor wooed?
Was ever woman in this humor won?
I'll have her, but I will not keep her long.
What, I that killed her husband and his father,
To take her in her heart's extremest hate,
With curses in her mouth, tears in her eyes,
The bleeding witness of my hatred by,
Having God, her conscience, and these bars against
me,
And I no friends to back my suit at all
But the plain devil and dissembling looks?
And yet to win her, all the world to nothing!
Ha!
Hath she forgot already that brave prince,
Edward, her lord, whom I some three months since
Stabbed in my angry mood at Tewkesbury?
A sweeter and a lovelier gentleman,
Framed in the prodigality of nature,
Young, valiant, wise, and, no doubt, right royal,
The spacious world cannot again afford.
And will she yet abase her eyes on me,
That cropped the golden prime of this sweet prince
And made her widow to a woeful bed?
On me, whose all not equals Edward's moiety?
On me, that halts and am misshapen thus?
My dukedom to a beggarly denier,
I do mistake my person all this while!
Upon my life, she finds, although I cannot,
Myself to be a marv'lous proper man.
I'll be at charges for a looking glass
And entertain a score or two of tailors
To study fashions to adorn my body.
Since I am crept in favor with myself,
I will maintain it with some little cost.
But first I'll turn yon fellow in his grave
And then return lamenting to my love.
Shine out, fair sun, till I have bought a glass,
That I may see my shadow as I pass.
fullText: No, to Whitefriars. There attend my coming. Was ever woman in this humor wooed? Was ever woman in this humor won? I'll have her, but I will not keep her long. What, I that killed her husband and his father, To take her in her heart's extremest hate, With curses in her mouth, tears in her eyes, The bleeding witness of my hatred by, Having God, her conscience, and these bars against me, And I no friends to back my suit at all But the plain devil and dissembling looks? And yet to win her, all the world to nothing! Ha! Hath she forgot already that brave prince, Edward, her lord, whom I some three months since Stabbed in my angry mood at Tewkesbury? A sweeter and a lovelier gentleman, Framed in the prodigality of nature, Young, valiant, wise, and, no doubt, right royal, The spacious world cannot again afford. And will she yet abase her eyes on me, That cropped the golden prime of this sweet prince And made her widow to a woeful bed? On me, whose all not equals Edward's moiety? On me, that halts and am misshapen thus? My dukedom to a beggarly denier, I do mistake my person all this while! Upon my life, she finds, although I cannot, Myself to be a marv'lous proper man. I'll be at charges for a looking glass And entertain a score or two of tailors To study fashions to adorn my body. Since I am crept in favor with myself, I will maintain it with some little cost. But first I'll turn yon fellow in his grave And then return lamenting to my love. Shine out, fair sun, till I have bought a glass, That I may see my shadow as I pass.

lines: They do me wrong, and I will not endure it!
Who is it that complains unto the King
That I, forsooth, am stern and love them not?
By holy Paul, they love his Grace but lightly
That fill his ears with such dissentious rumors.
Because I cannot flatter and look fair,
Smile in men's faces, smooth, deceive, and cog,
Duck with French nods and apish courtesy,
I must be held a rancorous enemy.
Cannot a plain man live and think no harm,
But thus his simple truth must be abused
With silken, sly, insinuating Jacks?
fullText: They do me wrong, and I will not endure it! Who is it that complains unto the King That I, forsooth, am stern and love them not? By holy Paul, they love his Grace but lightly That fill his ears with such dissentious rumors. Because I cannot flatter and look fair, Smile in men's faces, smooth, deceive, and cog, Duck with French nods and apish courtesy, I must be held a rancorous enemy. Cannot a plain man live and think no harm, But thus his simple truth must be abused With silken, sly, insinuating Jacks?

lines: To thee, that hast nor honesty nor grace.
When have I injured thee? When done thee
wrong?--
Or thee?--Or thee? Or any of your faction?
A plague upon you all! His royal Grace,
Whom God preserve better than you would wish,
Cannot be quiet scarce a breathing while
But you must trouble him with lewd complaints.
fullText: To thee, that hast nor honesty nor grace. When have I injured thee? When done thee wrong?-- Or thee?--Or thee? Or any of your faction? A plague upon you all! His royal Grace, Whom God preserve better than you would wish, Cannot be quiet scarce a breathing while But you must trouble him with lewd complaints.

lines: I cannot tell. The world is grown so bad
That wrens make prey where eagles dare not perch.
Since every Jack became a gentleman,
There's many a gentle person made a Jack.
fullText: I cannot tell. The world is grown so bad That wrens make prey where eagles dare not perch. Since every Jack became a gentleman, There's many a gentle person made a Jack.

lines: Meantime God grants that we have need of
you.
Our brother is imprisoned by your means,
Myself disgraced, and the nobility
Held in contempt, while great promotions
Are daily given to ennoble those
That scarce some two days since were worth a
noble.
fullText: Meantime God grants that we have need of you. Our brother is imprisoned by your means, Myself disgraced, and the nobility Held in contempt, while great promotions Are daily given to ennoble those That scarce some two days since were worth a noble.

lines: You may deny that you were not the mean
Of my Lord Hastings' late imprisonment.
fullText: You may deny that you were not the mean Of my Lord Hastings' late imprisonment.

lines: She may, Lord Rivers. Why, who knows not so?
She may do more, sir, than denying that.
She may help you to many fair preferments
And then deny her aiding hand therein,
And lay those honors on your high desert.
What may she not? She may, ay, marry, may she--
fullText: She may, Lord Rivers. Why, who knows not so? She may do more, sir, than denying that. She may help you to many fair preferments And then deny her aiding hand therein, And lay those honors on your high desert. What may she not? She may, ay, marry, may she--

lines: What, marry, may she? Marry with a king,
A bachelor, and a handsome stripling too.
Iwis, your grandam had a worser match.
fullText: What, marry, may she? Marry with a king, A bachelor, and a handsome stripling too. Iwis, your grandam had a worser match.

lines: What, threat you me with telling of the King?
Tell him and spare not. Look, what I have said,
I will avouch 't in presence of the King;
I dare adventure to be sent to th' Tower.
'Tis time to speak. My pains are quite forgot.
fullText: What, threat you me with telling of the King? Tell him and spare not. Look, what I have said, I will avouch 't in presence of the King; I dare adventure to be sent to th' Tower. 'Tis time to speak. My pains are quite forgot.

lines: Ere you were queen, ay, or your husband king,
I was a packhorse in his great affairs,
A weeder-out of his proud adversaries,
A liberal rewarder of his friends.
To royalize his blood, I spent mine own.
fullText: Ere you were queen, ay, or your husband king, I was a packhorse in his great affairs, A weeder-out of his proud adversaries, A liberal rewarder of his friends. To royalize his blood, I spent mine own.

lines: In all which time, you and your husband Grey
Were factious for the House of Lancaster.--
And, Rivers, so were you.--Was not your husband
In Margaret's battle at Saint Albans slain?
Let me put in your minds, if you forget,
What you have been ere this, and what you are;
Withal, what I have been, and what I am.
fullText: In all which time, you and your husband Grey Were factious for the House of Lancaster.-- And, Rivers, so were you.--Was not your husband In Margaret's battle at Saint Albans slain? Let me put in your minds, if you forget, What you have been ere this, and what you are; Withal, what I have been, and what I am.

lines: Poor Clarence did forsake his father Warwick,
Ay, and forswore himself--which Jesu pardon!--
fullText: Poor Clarence did forsake his father Warwick, Ay, and forswore himself--which Jesu pardon!--

lines: To fight on Edward's party for the crown;
And for his meed, poor lord, he is mewed up.
I would to God my heart were flint, like Edward's,
Or Edward's soft and pitiful, like mine.
I am too childish-foolish for this world.
fullText: To fight on Edward's party for the crown; And for his meed, poor lord, he is mewed up. I would to God my heart were flint, like Edward's, Or Edward's soft and pitiful, like mine. I am too childish-foolish for this world.

lines: If I should be? I had rather be a peddler.
Far be it from my heart, the thought thereof.
fullText: If I should be? I had rather be a peddler. Far be it from my heart, the thought thereof.

lines: Foul, wrinkled witch, what mak'st thou in my
sight?
fullText: Foul, wrinkled witch, what mak'st thou in my sight?

lines: Wert thou not banished on pain of death?
fullText: Wert thou not banished on pain of death?

lines: The curse my noble father laid on thee
When thou didst crown his warlike brows with
paper,
And with thy scorns drew'st rivers from his eyes,
And then, to dry them, gav'st the Duke a clout
Steeped in the faultless blood of pretty Rutland--
His curses then, from bitterness of soul
Denounced against thee, are all fall'n upon thee,
And God, not we, hath plagued thy bloody deed.
fullText: The curse my noble father laid on thee When thou didst crown his warlike brows with paper, And with thy scorns drew'st rivers from his eyes, And then, to dry them, gav'st the Duke a clout Steeped in the faultless blood of pretty Rutland-- His curses then, from bitterness of soul Denounced against thee, are all fall'n upon thee, And God, not we, hath plagued thy bloody deed.

lines: Have done thy charm, thou hateful, withered hag.
fullText: Have done thy charm, thou hateful, withered hag.

lines: Margaret.
fullText: Margaret.

lines: Ha?
fullText: Ha?

lines: I cry thee mercy, then, for I did think
That thou hadst called me all these bitter names.
fullText: I cry thee mercy, then, for I did think That thou hadst called me all these bitter names.

lines: 'Tis done by me and ends in "Margaret."
fullText: 'Tis done by me and ends in "Margaret."

lines: Good counsel, marry.--Learn it, learn it, marquess.
fullText: Good counsel, marry.--Learn it, learn it, marquess.

lines: Ay, and much more; but I was born so high.
Our aerie buildeth in the cedar's top,
And dallies with the wind and scorns the sun.
fullText: Ay, and much more; but I was born so high. Our aerie buildeth in the cedar's top, And dallies with the wind and scorns the sun.

lines: What doth she say, my lord of Buckingham?
fullText: What doth she say, my lord of Buckingham?

lines: I cannot blame her. By God's holy mother,
She hath had too much wrong, and I repent
My part thereof that I have done to her.
fullText: I cannot blame her. By God's holy mother, She hath had too much wrong, and I repent My part thereof that I have done to her.

lines: Yet you have all the vantage of her wrong.
I was too hot to do somebody good
That is too cold in thinking of it now.
Marry, as for Clarence, he is well repaid;
He is franked up to fatting for his pains.
God pardon them that are the cause thereof.
fullText: Yet you have all the vantage of her wrong. I was too hot to do somebody good That is too cold in thinking of it now. Marry, as for Clarence, he is well repaid; He is franked up to fatting for his pains. God pardon them that are the cause thereof.

lines: So do I ever-- being well advised,
For had I cursed now, I had cursed myself.
fullText: So do I ever-- being well advised, For had I cursed now, I had cursed myself.

lines: I do the wrong and first begin to brawl.
The secret mischiefs that I set abroach
I lay unto the grievous charge of others.
Clarence, who I indeed have cast in darkness,
I do beweep to many simple gulls,
Namely, to Derby, Hastings, Buckingham,
And tell them 'tis the Queen and her allies
That stir the King against the Duke my brother.
Now they believe it and withal whet me
To be revenged on Rivers, Dorset, Grey;
But then I sigh and, with a piece of scripture,
Tell them that God bids us do good for evil;
And thus I clothe my naked villainy
With odd old ends stol'n forth of Holy Writ,
And seem a saint when most I play the devil.
fullText: I do the wrong and first begin to brawl. The secret mischiefs that I set abroach I lay unto the grievous charge of others. Clarence, who I indeed have cast in darkness, I do beweep to many simple gulls, Namely, to Derby, Hastings, Buckingham, And tell them 'tis the Queen and her allies That stir the King against the Duke my brother. Now they believe it and withal whet me To be revenged on Rivers, Dorset, Grey; But then I sigh and, with a piece of scripture, Tell them that God bids us do good for evil; And thus I clothe my naked villainy With odd old ends stol'n forth of Holy Writ, And seem a saint when most I play the devil.

lines: Well thought upon. I have it here about me.
When you have done, repair to Crosby Place.
But, sirs, be sudden in the execution,
Withal obdurate; do not hear him plead,
For Clarence is well-spoken and perhaps
May move your hearts to pity if you mark him.
fullText: Well thought upon. I have it here about me. When you have done, repair to Crosby Place. But, sirs, be sudden in the execution, Withal obdurate; do not hear him plead, For Clarence is well-spoken and perhaps May move your hearts to pity if you mark him.

lines: Your eyes drop millstones when fools' eyes fall
tears.
I like you lads. About your business straight.
Go, go, dispatch.
fullText: Your eyes drop millstones when fools' eyes fall tears. I like you lads. About your business straight. Go, go, dispatch.

lines: Good morrow to my sovereign king and queen,
And, princely peers, a happy time of day.
fullText: Good morrow to my sovereign king and queen, And, princely peers, a happy time of day.

lines: A blessed labor, my most sovereign lord.
Among this princely heap, if any here
By false intelligence or wrong surmise
Hold me a foe,
If I unwittingly, or in my rage,
Have aught committed that is hardly borne
By any in this presence, I desire
To reconcile me to his friendly peace.
'Tis death to me to be at enmity;
I hate it, and desire all good men's love.
First, madam, I entreat true peace of you,
Which I will purchase with my duteous service;--
Of you, my noble cousin Buckingham,
If ever any grudge were lodged between us;--
Of you and you, Lord Rivers and of Dorset,
That all without desert have frowned on me;--
Of you, Lord Woodeville and Lord Scales;--of you,
Dukes, earls, lords, gentlemen; indeed, of all.
I do not know that Englishman alive
With whom my soul is any jot at odds
More than the infant that is born tonight.
I thank my God for my humility.
fullText: A blessed labor, my most sovereign lord. Among this princely heap, if any here By false intelligence or wrong surmise Hold me a foe, If I unwittingly, or in my rage, Have aught committed that is hardly borne By any in this presence, I desire To reconcile me to his friendly peace. 'Tis death to me to be at enmity; I hate it, and desire all good men's love. First, madam, I entreat true peace of you, Which I will purchase with my duteous service;-- Of you, my noble cousin Buckingham, If ever any grudge were lodged between us;-- Of you and you, Lord Rivers and of Dorset, That all without desert have frowned on me;-- Of you, Lord Woodeville and Lord Scales;--of you, Dukes, earls, lords, gentlemen; indeed, of all. I do not know that Englishman alive With whom my soul is any jot at odds More than the infant that is born tonight. I thank my God for my humility.

lines: Why, madam, have I offered love for this,
To be so flouted in this royal presence?
Who knows not that the gentle duke is dead?
You do him injury to scorn his corse.
fullText: Why, madam, have I offered love for this, To be so flouted in this royal presence? Who knows not that the gentle duke is dead? You do him injury to scorn his corse.

lines: But he, poor man, by your first order died,
And that a winged Mercury did bear.
Some tardy cripple bare the countermand,
That came too lag to see him buried.
God grant that some, less noble and less loyal,
Nearer in bloody thoughts, and not in blood,
Deserve not worse than wretched Clarence did,
And yet go current from suspicion.
fullText: But he, poor man, by your first order died, And that a winged Mercury did bear. Some tardy cripple bare the countermand, That came too lag to see him buried. God grant that some, less noble and less loyal, Nearer in bloody thoughts, and not in blood, Deserve not worse than wretched Clarence did, And yet go current from suspicion.

lines: This is the fruits of rashness. Marked you not
How that the guilty kindred of the Queen
Looked pale when they did hear of Clarence' death?
O, they did urge it still unto the King.
God will revenge it. Come, lords, will you go
To comfort Edward with our company?
fullText: This is the fruits of rashness. Marked you not How that the guilty kindred of the Queen Looked pale when they did hear of Clarence' death? O, they did urge it still unto the King. God will revenge it. Come, lords, will you go To comfort Edward with our company?

lines: Sister, have comfort. All of us have cause
To wail the dimming of our shining star,
But none can help our harms by wailing them.--
Madam my mother, I do cry you mercy;
I did not see your Grace. Humbly on my knee
I crave your blessing.
fullText: Sister, have comfort. All of us have cause To wail the dimming of our shining star, But none can help our harms by wailing them.-- Madam my mother, I do cry you mercy; I did not see your Grace. Humbly on my knee I crave your blessing.

lines: Amen. And make me die a good old man!
That is the butt end of a mother's blessing;
I marvel that her Grace did leave it out.
fullText: Amen. And make me die a good old man! That is the butt end of a mother's blessing; I marvel that her Grace did leave it out.

lines: I hope the King made peace with all of us;
And the compact is firm and true in me.
fullText: I hope the King made peace with all of us; And the compact is firm and true in me.

lines: Then be it so, and go we to determine
Who they shall be that straight shall post to
Ludlow.--
Madam, and you, my sister, will you go
To give your censures in this business?
fullText: Then be it so, and go we to determine Who they shall be that straight shall post to Ludlow.-- Madam, and you, my sister, will you go To give your censures in this business?

lines: My other self, my council's consistory,
My oracle, my prophet, my dear cousin,
I, as a child, will go by thy direction.
Toward Ludlow then, for we'll not stay behind.
fullText: My other self, my council's consistory, My oracle, my prophet, my dear cousin, I, as a child, will go by thy direction. Toward Ludlow then, for we'll not stay behind.

lines: Welcome, dear cousin, my thoughts' sovereign.
The weary way hath made you melancholy.
fullText: Welcome, dear cousin, my thoughts' sovereign. The weary way hath made you melancholy.

lines: Sweet prince, the untainted virtue of your years
Hath not yet dived into the world's deceit;
Nor more can you distinguish of a man
Than of his outward show, which, God He knows,
Seldom or never jumpeth with the heart.
Those uncles which you want were dangerous.
Your Grace attended to their sugared words
But looked not on the poison of their hearts.
God keep you from them, and from such false
friends.
fullText: Sweet prince, the untainted virtue of your years Hath not yet dived into the world's deceit; Nor more can you distinguish of a man Than of his outward show, which, God He knows, Seldom or never jumpeth with the heart. Those uncles which you want were dangerous. Your Grace attended to their sugared words But looked not on the poison of their hearts. God keep you from them, and from such false friends.

lines: My lord, the Mayor of London comes to greet you.
fullText: My lord, the Mayor of London comes to greet you.

lines: Where it seems best unto your royal self.
If I may counsel you, some day or two
Your Highness shall repose you at the Tower;
Then where you please and shall be thought most fit
For your best health and recreation.
fullText: Where it seems best unto your royal self. If I may counsel you, some day or two Your Highness shall repose you at the Tower; Then where you please and shall be thought most fit For your best health and recreation.

lines: So wise so young, they say, do never live long.
fullText: So wise so young, they say, do never live long.

lines: I say, without characters fame lives long.
I moralize two meanings in one word.
fullText: I say, without characters fame lives long. I moralize two meanings in one word.

lines: Short summers lightly have a forward spring.
fullText: Short summers lightly have a forward spring.

lines: How fares our cousin, noble lord of York?
fullText: How fares our cousin, noble lord of York?

lines: He hath, my lord.
fullText: He hath, my lord.

lines: O my fair cousin, I must not say so.
fullText: O my fair cousin, I must not say so.

lines: He may command me as my sovereign,
But you have power in me as in a kinsman.
fullText: He may command me as my sovereign, But you have power in me as in a kinsman.

lines: My dagger, little cousin? With all my heart.
fullText: My dagger, little cousin? With all my heart.

lines: A greater gift than that I'll give my cousin.
fullText: A greater gift than that I'll give my cousin.

lines: Ay, gentle cousin, were it light enough.
fullText: Ay, gentle cousin, were it light enough.

lines: It is too heavy for your Grace to wear.
fullText: It is too heavy for your Grace to wear.

lines: What, would you have my weapon, little lord?
fullText: What, would you have my weapon, little lord?

lines: How?
fullText: How?

lines: My lord, will 't please you pass along?
Myself and my good cousin Buckingham
Will to your mother, to entreat of her
To meet you at the Tower and welcome you.
fullText: My lord, will 't please you pass along? Myself and my good cousin Buckingham Will to your mother, to entreat of her To meet you at the Tower and welcome you.

lines: Why, what should you fear?
fullText: Why, what should you fear?

lines: Nor none that live, I hope.
fullText: Nor none that live, I hope.

lines: No doubt, no doubt. O, 'tis a parlous boy,
Bold, quick, ingenious, forward, capable.
He is all the mother's, from the top to toe.
fullText: No doubt, no doubt. O, 'tis a parlous boy, Bold, quick, ingenious, forward, capable. He is all the mother's, from the top to toe.

lines: Commend me to Lord William. Tell him, Catesby,
His ancient knot of dangerous adversaries
Tomorrow are let blood at Pomfret Castle,
And bid my lord, for joy of this good news,
Give Mistress Shore one gentle kiss the more.
fullText: Commend me to Lord William. Tell him, Catesby, His ancient knot of dangerous adversaries Tomorrow are let blood at Pomfret Castle, And bid my lord, for joy of this good news, Give Mistress Shore one gentle kiss the more.

lines: Shall we hear from you, Catesby, ere we sleep?
fullText: Shall we hear from you, Catesby, ere we sleep?

lines: At Crosby House, there shall you find us both.
fullText: At Crosby House, there shall you find us both.

lines: Chop off his head. Something we will determine.
And look when I am king, claim thou of me
The earldom of Hereford, and all the movables
Whereof the King my brother was possessed.
fullText: Chop off his head. Something we will determine. And look when I am king, claim thou of me The earldom of Hereford, and all the movables Whereof the King my brother was possessed.

lines: And look to have it yielded with all kindness.
Come, let us sup betimes, that afterwards
We may digest our complots in some form.
fullText: And look to have it yielded with all kindness. Come, let us sup betimes, that afterwards We may digest our complots in some form.

lines: My noble lords and cousins all, good morrow.
I have been long a sleeper; but I trust
My absence doth neglect no great design
Which by my presence might have been concluded.
fullText: My noble lords and cousins all, good morrow. I have been long a sleeper; but I trust My absence doth neglect no great design Which by my presence might have been concluded.

lines: Than my Lord Hastings no man might be bolder.
His Lordship knows me well and loves me well.--
My lord of Ely, when I was last in Holborn
I saw good strawberries in your garden there;
I do beseech you, send for some of them.
fullText: Than my Lord Hastings no man might be bolder. His Lordship knows me well and loves me well.-- My lord of Ely, when I was last in Holborn I saw good strawberries in your garden there; I do beseech you, send for some of them.

lines: Cousin of Buckingham, a word with you.
Catesby hath sounded Hastings in our business
And finds the testy gentleman so hot
That he will lose his head ere give consent
His master's child, as worshipfully he terms it,
Shall lose the royalty of England's throne.
fullText: Cousin of Buckingham, a word with you. Catesby hath sounded Hastings in our business And finds the testy gentleman so hot That he will lose his head ere give consent His master's child, as worshipfully he terms it, Shall lose the royalty of England's throne.

lines: I pray you all, tell me what they deserve
That do conspire my death with devilish plots
Of damned witchcraft, and that have prevailed
Upon my body with their hellish charms?
fullText: I pray you all, tell me what they deserve That do conspire my death with devilish plots Of damned witchcraft, and that have prevailed Upon my body with their hellish charms?

lines: Then be your eyes the witness of their evil.
Look how I am bewitched! Behold mine arm
Is like a blasted sapling withered up;
And this is Edward's wife, that monstrous witch,
Consorted with that harlot, strumpet Shore,
That by their witchcraft thus have marked me.
fullText: Then be your eyes the witness of their evil. Look how I am bewitched! Behold mine arm Is like a blasted sapling withered up; And this is Edward's wife, that monstrous witch, Consorted with that harlot, strumpet Shore, That by their witchcraft thus have marked me.

lines: If? Thou protector of this damned strumpet,
Talk'st thou to me of "ifs"? Thou art a traitor.--
Off with his head. Now by Saint Paul I swear
I will not dine until I see the same.--
Lovell and Ratcliffe, look that it be done.--
The rest that love me, rise and follow me.
with the Lord Hastings.]
fullText: If? Thou protector of this damned strumpet, Talk'st thou to me of "ifs"? Thou art a traitor.-- Off with his head. Now by Saint Paul I swear I will not dine until I see the same.-- Lovell and Ratcliffe, look that it be done.-- The rest that love me, rise and follow me. with the Lord Hastings.]

lines: Come, cousin, canst thou quake and change thy
color,
Murder thy breath in middle of a word,
And then again begin, and stop again,
As if thou were distraught and mad with terror?
fullText: Come, cousin, canst thou quake and change thy color, Murder thy breath in middle of a word, And then again begin, and stop again, As if thou were distraught and mad with terror?

lines: He is; and see he brings the Mayor along.
fullText: He is; and see he brings the Mayor along.

lines: Look to the drawbridge there!
fullText: Look to the drawbridge there!

lines: Catesby, o'erlook the walls.
fullText: Catesby, o'erlook the walls.

lines: Look back! Defend thee! Here are enemies.
fullText: Look back! Defend thee! Here are enemies.

lines: Be patient. They are friends, Ratcliffe and Lovell.
fullText: Be patient. They are friends, Ratcliffe and Lovell.

lines: So dear I loved the man that I must weep.
I took him for the plainest harmless creature
That breathed upon the Earth a Christian;
Made him my book, wherein my soul recorded
The history of all her secret thoughts.
So smooth he daubed his vice with show of virtue
That, his apparent open guilt omitted--
I mean his conversation with Shore's wife--
He lived from all attainder of suspects.
fullText: So dear I loved the man that I must weep. I took him for the plainest harmless creature That breathed upon the Earth a Christian; Made him my book, wherein my soul recorded The history of all her secret thoughts. So smooth he daubed his vice with show of virtue That, his apparent open guilt omitted-- I mean his conversation with Shore's wife-- He lived from all attainder of suspects.

lines: What, think you we are Turks or infidels?
Or that we would, against the form of law,
Proceed thus rashly in the villain's death,
But that the extreme peril of the case,
The peace of England, and our persons' safety
Enforced us to this execution?
fullText: What, think you we are Turks or infidels? Or that we would, against the form of law, Proceed thus rashly in the villain's death, But that the extreme peril of the case, The peace of England, and our persons' safety Enforced us to this execution?

lines: And to that end we wished your Lordship here,
T' avoid the censures of the carping world.
fullText: And to that end we wished your Lordship here, T' avoid the censures of the carping world.

lines: Go after, after, cousin Buckingham.
The Mayor towards Guildhall hies him in all post.
There, at your meetest vantage of the time,
Infer the bastardy of Edward's children.
Tell them how Edward put to death a citizen
Only for saying he would make his son
Heir to the Crown--meaning indeed his house,
Which, by the sign thereof, was termed so.
Moreover, urge his hateful luxury
And bestial appetite in change of lust,
Which stretched unto their servants, daughters,
wives,
Even where his raging eye or savage heart,
Without control, lusted to make a prey.
Nay, for a need, thus far come near my person:
Tell them when that my mother went with child
Of that insatiate Edward, noble York
My princely father then had wars in France,
And, by true computation of the time,
Found that the issue was not his begot,
Which well appeared in his lineaments,
Being nothing like the noble duke my father.
Yet touch this sparingly, as 'twere far off,
Because, my lord, you know my mother lives.
fullText: Go after, after, cousin Buckingham. The Mayor towards Guildhall hies him in all post. There, at your meetest vantage of the time, Infer the bastardy of Edward's children. Tell them how Edward put to death a citizen Only for saying he would make his son Heir to the Crown--meaning indeed his house, Which, by the sign thereof, was termed so. Moreover, urge his hateful luxury And bestial appetite in change of lust, Which stretched unto their servants, daughters, wives, Even where his raging eye or savage heart, Without control, lusted to make a prey. Nay, for a need, thus far come near my person: Tell them when that my mother went with child Of that insatiate Edward, noble York My princely father then had wars in France, And, by true computation of the time, Found that the issue was not his begot, Which well appeared in his lineaments, Being nothing like the noble duke my father. Yet touch this sparingly, as 'twere far off, Because, my lord, you know my mother lives.

lines: If you thrive well, bring them to Baynard's Castle,
Where you shall find me well accompanied
With reverend fathers and well-learned bishops.
fullText: If you thrive well, bring them to Baynard's Castle, Where you shall find me well accompanied With reverend fathers and well-learned bishops.

lines: Go, Lovell, with all speed to Doctor Shaa.
both
Meet me within this hour at Baynard's Castle.
Now will I go to take some privy order
To draw the brats of Clarence out of sight,
And to give order that no manner person
Have any time recourse unto the Princes.
fullText: Go, Lovell, with all speed to Doctor Shaa. both Meet me within this hour at Baynard's Castle. Now will I go to take some privy order To draw the brats of Clarence out of sight, And to give order that no manner person Have any time recourse unto the Princes.

lines: How now, how now? What say the citizens?
fullText: How now, how now? What say the citizens?

lines: Touched you the bastardy of Edward's children?
fullText: Touched you the bastardy of Edward's children?

lines: And did they so?
fullText: And did they so?

lines: What tongueless blocks were they! Would they not
speak?
Will not the Mayor then and his brethren come?
fullText: What tongueless blocks were they! Would they not speak? Will not the Mayor then and his brethren come?

lines: I go. An if you plead as well for them
As I can say "nay" to thee for myself,
No doubt we bring it to a happy issue.
fullText: I go. An if you plead as well for them As I can say "nay" to thee for myself, No doubt we bring it to a happy issue.

lines: My lord, there needs no such apology.
I do beseech your Grace to pardon me,
Who, earnest in the service of my God,
Deferred the visitation of my friends.
But, leaving this, what is your Grace's pleasure?
fullText: My lord, there needs no such apology. I do beseech your Grace to pardon me, Who, earnest in the service of my God, Deferred the visitation of my friends. But, leaving this, what is your Grace's pleasure?

lines: I do suspect I have done some offense
That seems disgracious in the city's eye,
And that you come to reprehend my ignorance.
fullText: I do suspect I have done some offense That seems disgracious in the city's eye, And that you come to reprehend my ignorance.

lines: Else wherefore breathe I in a Christian land?
fullText: Else wherefore breathe I in a Christian land?

lines: I cannot tell if to depart in silence
Or bitterly to speak in your reproof
Best fitteth my degree or your condition.
If not to answer, you might haply think
Tongue-tied ambition, not replying, yielded
To bear the golden yoke of sovereignty,
Which fondly you would here impose on me.
If to reprove you for this suit of yours,
So seasoned with your faithful love to me,
Then on the other side I checked my friends.
Therefore, to speak, and to avoid the first,
And then, in speaking, not to incur the last,
Definitively thus I answer you:
Your love deserves my thanks, but my desert
Unmeritable shuns your high request.
First, if all obstacles were cut away
And that my path were even to the crown
As the ripe revenue and due of birth,
Yet so much is my poverty of spirit,
So mighty and so many my defects,
That I would rather hide me from my greatness,
Being a bark to brook no mighty sea,
Than in my greatness covet to be hid
And in the vapor of my glory smothered.
But, God be thanked, there is no need of me,
And much I need to help you, were there need.
The royal tree hath left us royal fruit,
Which, mellowed by the stealing hours of time,
Will well become the seat of majesty,
And make, no doubt, us happy by his reign.
On him I lay that you would lay on me,
The right and fortune of his happy stars,
Which God defend that I should wring from him.
fullText: I cannot tell if to depart in silence Or bitterly to speak in your reproof Best fitteth my degree or your condition. If not to answer, you might haply think Tongue-tied ambition, not replying, yielded To bear the golden yoke of sovereignty, Which fondly you would here impose on me. If to reprove you for this suit of yours, So seasoned with your faithful love to me, Then on the other side I checked my friends. Therefore, to speak, and to avoid the first, And then, in speaking, not to incur the last, Definitively thus I answer you: Your love deserves my thanks, but my desert Unmeritable shuns your high request. First, if all obstacles were cut away And that my path were even to the crown As the ripe revenue and due of birth, Yet so much is my poverty of spirit, So mighty and so many my defects, That I would rather hide me from my greatness, Being a bark to brook no mighty sea, Than in my greatness covet to be hid And in the vapor of my glory smothered. But, God be thanked, there is no need of me, And much I need to help you, were there need. The royal tree hath left us royal fruit, Which, mellowed by the stealing hours of time, Will well become the seat of majesty, And make, no doubt, us happy by his reign. On him I lay that you would lay on me, The right and fortune of his happy stars, Which God defend that I should wring from him.

lines: Alas, why would you heap this care on me?
I am unfit for state and majesty.
I do beseech you, take it not amiss;
I cannot, nor I will not, yield to you.
fullText: Alas, why would you heap this care on me? I am unfit for state and majesty. I do beseech you, take it not amiss; I cannot, nor I will not, yield to you.

lines: O, do not swear, my lord of Buckingham!
fullText: O, do not swear, my lord of Buckingham!

lines: Will you enforce me to a world of cares?
Call them again. I am not made of stones,
But penetrable to your kind entreaties,
Albeit against my conscience and my soul.
fullText: Will you enforce me to a world of cares? Call them again. I am not made of stones, But penetrable to your kind entreaties, Albeit against my conscience and my soul.

lines: In saying so, you shall but say the truth.
fullText: In saying so, you shall but say the truth.

lines: Even when you please, for you will have it so.
fullText: Even when you please, for you will have it so.

lines: Come, let us to our holy work again.--
Farewell, my cousin. Farewell, gentle friends.
fullText: Come, let us to our holy work again.-- Farewell, my cousin. Farewell, gentle friends.

lines: Stand all apart.--Cousin of Buckingham.
fullText: Stand all apart.--Cousin of Buckingham.

lines: Give me thy hand.
Thus high, by thy advice
And thy assistance is King Richard seated.
But shall we wear these glories for a day,
Or shall they last and we rejoice in them?
fullText: Give me thy hand. Thus high, by thy advice And thy assistance is King Richard seated. But shall we wear these glories for a day, Or shall they last and we rejoice in them?

lines: Ah, Buckingham, now do I play the touch,
To try if thou be current gold indeed:
Young Edward lives; think now what I would speak.
fullText: Ah, Buckingham, now do I play the touch, To try if thou be current gold indeed: Young Edward lives; think now what I would speak.

lines: Why, Buckingham, I say I would be king.
fullText: Why, Buckingham, I say I would be king.

lines: Ha! Am I king? 'Tis so--but Edward lives.
fullText: Ha! Am I king? 'Tis so--but Edward lives.

lines: O bitter consequence
That Edward still should live "true noble prince"!
Cousin, thou wast not wont to be so dull.
Shall I be plain? I wish the bastards dead,
And I would have it suddenly performed.
What sayst thou now? Speak suddenly. Be brief.
fullText: O bitter consequence That Edward still should live "true noble prince"! Cousin, thou wast not wont to be so dull. Shall I be plain? I wish the bastards dead, And I would have it suddenly performed. What sayst thou now? Speak suddenly. Be brief.

lines: Tut, tut, thou art all ice; thy kindness freezes.
Say, have I thy consent that they shall die?
fullText: Tut, tut, thou art all ice; thy kindness freezes. Say, have I thy consent that they shall die?

lines: I will converse with iron-witted fools
And unrespective boys. None are for me
That look into me with considerate eyes.
High-reaching Buckingham grows circumspect.--
Boy!
fullText: I will converse with iron-witted fools And unrespective boys. None are for me That look into me with considerate eyes. High-reaching Buckingham grows circumspect.-- Boy!

lines: Know'st thou not any whom corrupting gold
Will tempt unto a close exploit of death?
fullText: Know'st thou not any whom corrupting gold Will tempt unto a close exploit of death?

lines: What is his name?
fullText: What is his name?

lines: I partly know the man. Go, call him hither, boy.
No more shall be the neighbor to my counsels.
Hath he so long held out with me, untired,
And stops he now for breath? Well, be it so.
fullText: I partly know the man. Go, call him hither, boy. No more shall be the neighbor to my counsels. Hath he so long held out with me, untired, And stops he now for breath? Well, be it so.

lines: Come hither, Catesby. Rumor it abroad
That Anne my wife is very grievous sick.
I will take order for her keeping close.
Inquire me out some mean poor gentleman,
Whom I will marry straight to Clarence' daughter.
The boy is foolish, and I fear not him.
Look how thou dream'st! I say again, give out
That Anne my queen is sick and like to die.
About it, for it stands me much upon
To stop all hopes whose growth may damage me.
Or else my kingdom stands on brittle glass.
Murder her brothers, and then marry her--
Uncertain way of gain. But I am in
So far in blood that sin will pluck on sin.
Tear-falling pity dwells not in this eye.
fullText: Come hither, Catesby. Rumor it abroad That Anne my wife is very grievous sick. I will take order for her keeping close. Inquire me out some mean poor gentleman, Whom I will marry straight to Clarence' daughter. The boy is foolish, and I fear not him. Look how thou dream'st! I say again, give out That Anne my queen is sick and like to die. About it, for it stands me much upon To stop all hopes whose growth may damage me. Or else my kingdom stands on brittle glass. Murder her brothers, and then marry her-- Uncertain way of gain. But I am in So far in blood that sin will pluck on sin. Tear-falling pity dwells not in this eye.

lines: Art thou indeed?
fullText: Art thou indeed?

lines: Dar'st thou resolve to kill a friend of mine?
fullText: Dar'st thou resolve to kill a friend of mine?

lines: Why then, thou hast it. Two deep enemies,
Foes to my rest, and my sweet sleep's disturbers,
Are they that I would have thee deal upon.
Tyrrel, I mean those bastards in the Tower.
fullText: Why then, thou hast it. Two deep enemies, Foes to my rest, and my sweet sleep's disturbers, Are they that I would have thee deal upon. Tyrrel, I mean those bastards in the Tower.

lines: Thou sing'st sweet music. Hark, come hither, Tyrrel.
Go, by this token. Rise, and lend thine ear.
to him. Then Tyrrel steps back.]
There is no more but so. Say it is done,
And I will love thee and prefer thee for it.
fullText: Thou sing'st sweet music. Hark, come hither, Tyrrel. Go, by this token. Rise, and lend thine ear. to him. Then Tyrrel steps back.] There is no more but so. Say it is done, And I will love thee and prefer thee for it.

lines: Well, let that rest. Dorset is fled to Richmond.
fullText: Well, let that rest. Dorset is fled to Richmond.

lines: Stanley, he is your wife's son. Well, look unto it.
fullText: Stanley, he is your wife's son. Well, look unto it.

lines: Stanley, look to your wife. If she convey
Letters to Richmond, you shall answer it.
fullText: Stanley, look to your wife. If she convey Letters to Richmond, you shall answer it.

lines: I do remember me, Henry the Sixth
Did prophesy that Richmond should be king,
When Richmond was a little peevish boy.
A king perhaps--
fullText: I do remember me, Henry the Sixth Did prophesy that Richmond should be king, When Richmond was a little peevish boy. A king perhaps--

lines: How chance the prophet could not at that time
Have told me, I being by, that I should kill him?
fullText: How chance the prophet could not at that time Have told me, I being by, that I should kill him?

lines: Richmond! When last I was at Exeter,
The Mayor in courtesy showed me the castle
And called it Rougemont, at which name I started,
Because a bard of Ireland told me once
I should not live long after I saw Richmond.
fullText: Richmond! When last I was at Exeter, The Mayor in courtesy showed me the castle And called it Rougemont, at which name I started, Because a bard of Ireland told me once I should not live long after I saw Richmond.

lines: Ay, what's o'clock?
fullText: Ay, what's o'clock?

lines: Well, but what's o'clock?
fullText: Well, but what's o'clock?

lines: Well, let it strike.
fullText: Well, let it strike.

lines: Because that, like a jack, thou keep'st the stroke
Betwixt thy begging and my meditation.
I am not in the giving vein today.
fullText: Because that, like a jack, thou keep'st the stroke Betwixt thy begging and my meditation. I am not in the giving vein today.

lines: Thou troublest me; I am not in the vein.
fullText: Thou troublest me; I am not in the vein.

lines: Kind Tyrrel, am I happy in thy news?
fullText: Kind Tyrrel, am I happy in thy news?

lines: But did'st thou see them dead?
fullText: But did'st thou see them dead?

lines: And buried, gentle Tyrrel?
fullText: And buried, gentle Tyrrel?

lines: Come to me, Tyrrel, soon at after-supper,
When thou shalt tell the process of their death.
Meantime, but think how I may do thee good,
And be inheritor of thy desire.
Farewell till then.
fullText: Come to me, Tyrrel, soon at after-supper, When thou shalt tell the process of their death. Meantime, but think how I may do thee good, And be inheritor of thy desire. Farewell till then.

lines: The son of Clarence have I pent up close,
His daughter meanly have I matched in marriage,
The sons of Edward sleep in Abraham's bosom,
And Anne my wife hath bid this world goodnight.
Now, for I know the Breton Richmond aims
At young Elizabeth, my brother's daughter,
And by that knot looks proudly on the crown,
To her go I, a jolly thriving wooer.
fullText: The son of Clarence have I pent up close, His daughter meanly have I matched in marriage, The sons of Edward sleep in Abraham's bosom, And Anne my wife hath bid this world goodnight. Now, for I know the Breton Richmond aims At young Elizabeth, my brother's daughter, And by that knot looks proudly on the crown, To her go I, a jolly thriving wooer.

lines: Good or bad news, that thou com'st in so bluntly?
fullText: Good or bad news, that thou com'st in so bluntly?

lines: Ely with Richmond troubles me more near
Than Buckingham and his rash-levied strength.
Come, I have learned that fearful commenting
Is leaden servitor to dull delay;
Delay leads impotent and snail-paced beggary;
Then fiery expedition be my wing,
Jove's Mercury, and herald for a king.
Go, muster men. My counsel is my shield.
We must be brief when traitors brave the field.
fullText: Ely with Richmond troubles me more near Than Buckingham and his rash-levied strength. Come, I have learned that fearful commenting Is leaden servitor to dull delay; Delay leads impotent and snail-paced beggary; Then fiery expedition be my wing, Jove's Mercury, and herald for a king. Go, muster men. My counsel is my shield. We must be brief when traitors brave the field.

lines: Who intercepts me in my expedition?
fullText: Who intercepts me in my expedition?

lines: A flourish, trumpets! Strike alarum, drums!
Let not the heavens hear these telltale women
Rail on the Lord's anointed. Strike, I say!
Either be patient and entreat me fair,
Or with the clamorous report of war
Thus will I drown your exclamations.
fullText: A flourish, trumpets! Strike alarum, drums! Let not the heavens hear these telltale women Rail on the Lord's anointed. Strike, I say! Either be patient and entreat me fair, Or with the clamorous report of war Thus will I drown your exclamations.

lines: Ay, I thank God, my father, and yourself.
fullText: Ay, I thank God, my father, and yourself.

lines: Madam, I have a touch of your condition,
That cannot brook the accent of reproof.
fullText: Madam, I have a touch of your condition, That cannot brook the accent of reproof.

lines: Do then, but I'll not hear.
fullText: Do then, but I'll not hear.

lines: And brief, good mother, for I am in haste.
fullText: And brief, good mother, for I am in haste.

lines: And came I not at last to comfort you?
fullText: And came I not at last to comfort you?

lines: Faith, none but Humfrey Hower, that called your
Grace
To breakfast once, forth of my company.
If I be so disgracious in your eye,
Let me march on and not offend you, madam.--
Strike up the drum.
fullText: Faith, none but Humfrey Hower, that called your Grace To breakfast once, forth of my company. If I be so disgracious in your eye, Let me march on and not offend you, madam.-- Strike up the drum.

lines: You speak too bitterly.
fullText: You speak too bitterly.

lines: So.
fullText: So.

lines: Stay, madam. I must talk a word with you.
fullText: Stay, madam. I must talk a word with you.

lines: You have a daughter called Elizabeth,
Virtuous and fair, royal and gracious.
fullText: You have a daughter called Elizabeth, Virtuous and fair, royal and gracious.

lines: Wrong not her birth. She is a royal princess.
fullText: Wrong not her birth. She is a royal princess.

lines: Her life is safest only in her birth.
fullText: Her life is safest only in her birth.

lines: Lo, at their birth good stars were opposite.
fullText: Lo, at their birth good stars were opposite.

lines: All unavoided is the doom of destiny.
fullText: All unavoided is the doom of destiny.

lines: You speak as if that I had slain my cousins.
fullText: You speak as if that I had slain my cousins.

lines: Madam, so thrive I in my enterprise
And dangerous success of bloody wars
As I intend more good to you and yours
Than ever you or yours by me were harmed!
fullText: Madam, so thrive I in my enterprise And dangerous success of bloody wars As I intend more good to you and yours Than ever you or yours by me were harmed!

lines: Th' advancement of your children, gentle lady.
fullText: Th' advancement of your children, gentle lady.

lines: Unto the dignity and height of fortune,
The high imperial type of this Earth's glory.
fullText: Unto the dignity and height of fortune, The high imperial type of this Earth's glory.

lines: Even all I have--ay, and myself and all--
Will I withal endow a child of thine;
So in the Lethe of thy angry soul
Thou drown the sad remembrance of those wrongs
Which thou supposest I have done to thee.
fullText: Even all I have--ay, and myself and all-- Will I withal endow a child of thine; So in the Lethe of thy angry soul Thou drown the sad remembrance of those wrongs Which thou supposest I have done to thee.

lines: Then know that from my soul I love thy daughter.
fullText: Then know that from my soul I love thy daughter.

lines: What do you think?
fullText: What do you think?

lines: Be not so hasty to confound my meaning.
I mean that with my soul I love thy daughter
And do intend to make her Queen of England.
fullText: Be not so hasty to confound my meaning. I mean that with my soul I love thy daughter And do intend to make her Queen of England.

lines: Even he that makes her queen. Who else should be?
fullText: Even he that makes her queen. Who else should be?

lines: Even so. How think you of it?
fullText: Even so. How think you of it?

lines: That would I learn of you,
As one being best acquainted with her humor.
fullText: That would I learn of you, As one being best acquainted with her humor.

lines: Madam, with all my heart.
fullText: Madam, with all my heart.

lines: You mock me, madam. This is not the way
To win your daughter.
fullText: You mock me, madam. This is not the way To win your daughter.

lines: Say that I did all this for love of her.
fullText: Say that I did all this for love of her.

lines: Look what is done cannot be now amended.
Men shall deal unadvisedly sometimes,
Which after-hours gives leisure to repent.
If I did take the kingdom from your sons,
To make amends I'll give it to your daughter.
If I have killed the issue of your womb,
To quicken your increase I will beget
Mine issue of your blood upon your daughter.
A grandam's name is little less in love
Than is the doting title of a mother.
They are as children but one step below,
Even of your metal, of your very blood,
Of all one pain, save for a night of groans
Endured of her for whom you bid like sorrow.
Your children were vexation to your youth,
But mine shall be a comfort to your age.
The loss you have is but a son being king,
And by that loss your daughter is made queen.
I cannot make you what amends I would;
Therefore accept such kindness as I can.
Dorset your son, that with a fearful soul
Leads discontented steps in foreign soil,
This fair alliance quickly shall call home
To high promotions and great dignity.
The king that calls your beauteous daughter wife
Familiarly shall call thy Dorset brother.
Again shall you be mother to a king,
And all the ruins of distressful times
Repaired with double riches of content.
What, we have many goodly days to see!
The liquid drops of tears that you have shed
Shall come again, transformed to orient pearl,
Advantaging their love with interest
Of ten times double gain of happiness.
Go then, my mother; to thy daughter go.
Make bold her bashful years with your experience;
Prepare her ears to hear a wooer's tale;
Put in her tender heart th' aspiring flame
Of golden sovereignty; acquaint the Princess
With the sweet silent hours of marriage joys;
And when this arm of mine hath chastised
The petty rebel, dull-brained Buckingham,
Bound with triumphant garlands will I come
And lead thy daughter to a conqueror's bed,
To whom I will retail my conquest won,
And she shall be sole victoress, Caesar's Caesar.
