If I did take the kingdom from your sons, To make amends I'll give it to your daughter. If I have killed the issue of your womb, To quicken your increase I will beget Mine issue of your blood upon your daughter. A grandam's name is little less in love Than is the doting title of a mother. They are as children but one step below, Even of your metal, of your very blood, Of all one pain, save for a night of groans Endured of her for whom you bid like sorrow. Your children were vexation to your youth, But mine shall be a comfort to your age. The loss you have is but a son being king, And by that loss your daughter is made queen. I cannot make you what amends I would; Therefore accept such kindness as I can. Dorset your son, that with a fearful soul Leads discontented steps in foreign soil, This fair alliance quickly shall call home To high promotions and great dignity. The king that calls your beauteous daughter wife Familiarly shall call thy Dorset brother. Again shall you be mother to a king, And all the ruins of distressful times Repaired with double riches of content. What, we have many goodly days to see! The liquid drops of tears that you have shed Shall come again, transformed to orient pearl, Advantaging their love with interest Of ten times double gain of happiness. Go then, my mother; to thy daughter go. Make bold her bashful years with your experience; Prepare her ears to hear a wooer's tale; Put in her tender heart th' aspiring flame Of golden sovereignty; acquaint the Princess With the sweet silent hours of marriage joys; And when this arm of mine hath chastised The petty rebel, dull-brained Buckingham, Bound with triumphant garlands will I come And lead thy daughter to a conqueror's bed, To whom I will retail my conquest won, And she shall be sole victoress, Caesar's Caesar.

lines: Infer fair England's peace by this alliance.
fullText: Infer fair England's peace by this alliance.

lines: Tell her the King, that may command, entreats--
fullText: Tell her the King, that may command, entreats--

lines: Say she shall be a high and mighty queen.
fullText: Say she shall be a high and mighty queen.

lines: Say I will love her everlastingly.
fullText: Say I will love her everlastingly.

lines: Sweetly in force unto her fair life's end.
fullText: Sweetly in force unto her fair life's end.

lines: As long as heaven and nature lengthens it.
fullText: As long as heaven and nature lengthens it.

lines: Say I, her sovereign, am her subject low.
fullText: Say I, her sovereign, am her subject low.

lines: Be eloquent in my behalf to her.
fullText: Be eloquent in my behalf to her.

lines: Then plainly to her tell my loving tale.
fullText: Then plainly to her tell my loving tale.

lines: Your reasons are too shallow and too quick.
fullText: Your reasons are too shallow and too quick.

lines: Harp not on that string, madam; that is past.
fullText: Harp not on that string, madam; that is past.

lines: Now by my George, my Garter, and my crown--
fullText: Now by my George, my Garter, and my crown--

lines: I swear--
fullText: I swear--

lines: Then, by myself--
fullText: Then, by myself--

lines: Now, by the world--
fullText: Now, by the world--

lines: My father's death--
fullText: My father's death--

lines: Why then, by God.
fullText: Why then, by God.

lines: The time to come.
fullText: The time to come.

lines: As I intend to prosper and repent,
So thrive I in my dangerous affairs
Of hostile arms! Myself myself confound,
Heaven and fortune bar me happy hours,
Day, yield me not thy light, nor night thy rest,
Be opposite all planets of good luck
To my proceeding if, with dear heart's love,
Immaculate devotion, holy thoughts,
I tender not thy beauteous princely daughter.
In her consists my happiness and thine.
Without her follows to myself and thee,
Herself, the land, and many a Christian soul,
Death, desolation, ruin, and decay.
It cannot be avoided but by this;
It will not be avoided but by this.
Therefore, dear mother--I must call you so--
Be the attorney of my love to her;
Plead what I will be, not what I have been;
Not my deserts, but what I will deserve.
Urge the necessity and state of times,
And be not peevish found in great designs.
fullText: As I intend to prosper and repent, So thrive I in my dangerous affairs Of hostile arms! Myself myself confound, Heaven and fortune bar me happy hours, Day, yield me not thy light, nor night thy rest, Be opposite all planets of good luck To my proceeding if, with dear heart's love, Immaculate devotion, holy thoughts, I tender not thy beauteous princely daughter. In her consists my happiness and thine. Without her follows to myself and thee, Herself, the land, and many a Christian soul, Death, desolation, ruin, and decay. It cannot be avoided but by this; It will not be avoided but by this. Therefore, dear mother--I must call you so-- Be the attorney of my love to her; Plead what I will be, not what I have been; Not my deserts, but what I will deserve. Urge the necessity and state of times, And be not peevish found in great designs.

lines: Ay, if the devil tempt you to do good.
fullText: Ay, if the devil tempt you to do good.

lines: Ay, if your self's remembrance wrong yourself.
fullText: Ay, if your self's remembrance wrong yourself.

lines: But in your daughter's womb I bury them,
Where, in that nest of spicery, they will breed
Selves of themselves, to your recomforture.
fullText: But in your daughter's womb I bury them, Where, in that nest of spicery, they will breed Selves of themselves, to your recomforture.

lines: And be a happy mother by the deed.
fullText: And be a happy mother by the deed.

lines: Bear her my true love's kiss; and so, farewell.
Relenting fool and shallow, changing woman!
fullText: Bear her my true love's kiss; and so, farewell. Relenting fool and shallow, changing woman!

lines: Some light-foot friend post to the Duke of
Norfolk--
Ratcliffe thyself, or Catesby. Where is he?
fullText: Some light-foot friend post to the Duke of Norfolk-- Ratcliffe thyself, or Catesby. Where is he?

lines: Catesby, fly to the Duke.
fullText: Catesby, fly to the Duke.

lines: Ratcliffe, come hither. Post to Salisbury.When thou com'st thither-- Dull,
unmindful villain,
Why stay'st thou here and go'st not to the Duke?
fullText: Ratcliffe, come hither. Post to Salisbury.When thou com'st thither-- Dull, unmindful villain, Why stay'st thou here and go'st not to the Duke?

lines: O true, good Catesby. Bid him levy straight
The greatest strength and power that he can make
And meet me suddenly at Salisbury.
fullText: O true, good Catesby. Bid him levy straight The greatest strength and power that he can make And meet me suddenly at Salisbury.

lines: Why, what wouldst thou do there before I go?
fullText: Why, what wouldst thou do there before I go?

lines: My mind is changed.
fullText: My mind is changed.

lines: Hoyday, a riddle! Neither good nor bad.
What need'st thou run so many miles about
When thou mayst tell thy tale the nearest way?
Once more, what news?
fullText: Hoyday, a riddle! Neither good nor bad. What need'st thou run so many miles about When thou mayst tell thy tale the nearest way? Once more, what news?

lines: There let him sink, and be the seas on him!
White-livered runagate, what doth he there?
fullText: There let him sink, and be the seas on him! White-livered runagate, what doth he there?

lines: Well, as you guess?
fullText: Well, as you guess?

lines: Is the chair empty? Is the sword unswayed?
Is the King dead, the empire unpossessed?
What heir of York is there alive but we?
And who is England's king but great York's heir?
Then tell me, what makes he upon the seas?
fullText: Is the chair empty? Is the sword unswayed? Is the King dead, the empire unpossessed? What heir of York is there alive but we? And who is England's king but great York's heir? Then tell me, what makes he upon the seas?

lines: Unless for that he comes to be your liege,
You cannot guess wherefore the Welshman comes.
Thou wilt revolt and fly to him, I fear.
fullText: Unless for that he comes to be your liege, You cannot guess wherefore the Welshman comes. Thou wilt revolt and fly to him, I fear.

lines: Where is thy power, then, to beat him back?
Where be thy tenants and thy followers?
Are they not now upon the western shore,
Safe-conducting the rebels from their ships?
fullText: Where is thy power, then, to beat him back? Where be thy tenants and thy followers? Are they not now upon the western shore, Safe-conducting the rebels from their ships?

lines: Cold friends to me. What do they in the north
When they should serve their sovereign in the west?
fullText: Cold friends to me. What do they in the north When they should serve their sovereign in the west?

lines: Ay, thou wouldst be gone to join with Richmond,
But I'll not trust thee.
fullText: Ay, thou wouldst be gone to join with Richmond, But I'll not trust thee.

lines: Go then and muster men, but leave behind
Your son George Stanley. Look your heart be firm,
Or else his head's assurance is but frail.
fullText: Go then and muster men, but leave behind Your son George Stanley. Look your heart be firm, Or else his head's assurance is but frail.

lines: Out on you, owls! Nothing but songs of death.
There, take thou that till thou bring better news.
fullText: Out on you, owls! Nothing but songs of death. There, take thou that till thou bring better news.

lines: I cry thee mercy.
There is my purse to cure that blow of thine.
Hath any well-advised friend proclaimed
Reward to him that brings the traitor in?
fullText: I cry thee mercy. There is my purse to cure that blow of thine. Hath any well-advised friend proclaimed Reward to him that brings the traitor in?

lines: March on, march on, since we are up in arms,
If not to fight with foreign enemies,
Yet to beat down these rebels here at home.
fullText: March on, march on, since we are up in arms, If not to fight with foreign enemies, Yet to beat down these rebels here at home.

lines: Away towards Salisbury! While we reason here,
A royal battle might be won and lost.
Someone take order Buckingham be brought
To Salisbury. The rest march on with me.
fullText: Away towards Salisbury! While we reason here, A royal battle might be won and lost. Someone take order Buckingham be brought To Salisbury. The rest march on with me.

lines: Here pitch our tent, even here in Bosworth field.
My lord of Surrey, why look you so sad?
fullText: Here pitch our tent, even here in Bosworth field. My lord of Surrey, why look you so sad?

lines: My lord of Norfolk--
fullText: My lord of Norfolk--

lines: Norfolk, we must have knocks, ha, must we not?
fullText: Norfolk, we must have knocks, ha, must we not?

lines: Up with my tent!--Here will I lie tonight.
But where tomorrow? Well, all's one for that.
Who hath descried the number of the traitors?
fullText: Up with my tent!--Here will I lie tonight. But where tomorrow? Well, all's one for that. Who hath descried the number of the traitors?

lines: Why, our battalia trebles that account.
Besides, the King's name is a tower of strength
Which they upon the adverse faction want.--
Up with the tent!--Come, noble gentlemen,
Let us survey the vantage of the ground.
Call for some men of sound direction;
Let's lack no discipline, make no delay,
For, lords, tomorrow is a busy day.
fullText: Why, our battalia trebles that account. Besides, the King's name is a tower of strength Which they upon the adverse faction want.-- Up with the tent!--Come, noble gentlemen, Let us survey the vantage of the ground. Call for some men of sound direction; Let's lack no discipline, make no delay, For, lords, tomorrow is a busy day.

lines: What is 't o'clock?
fullText: What is 't o'clock?

lines: I will not sup tonight. Give me some ink and paper.
What, is my beaver easier than it was,
And all my armor laid into my tent?
fullText: I will not sup tonight. Give me some ink and paper. What, is my beaver easier than it was, And all my armor laid into my tent?

lines: Good Norfolk, hie thee to thy charge.
Use careful watch. Choose trusty sentinels.

lines: Sawst thou the melancholy Lord Northumberland?
fullText: Sawst thou the melancholy Lord Northumberland?

lines: So, I am satisfied. Give me a bowl of wine.
I have not that alacrity of spirit
Nor cheer of mind that I was wont to have.
Set it down. Is ink and paper ready?
fullText: So, I am satisfied. Give me a bowl of wine. I have not that alacrity of spirit Nor cheer of mind that I was wont to have. Set it down. Is ink and paper ready?

lines: Bid my guard watch. Leave me.
Ratcliffe, about the mid of night come to my tent
And help to arm me. Leave me, I say.
which is guarded by Soldiers.]

lines: Give me another horse! Bind up my wounds!
Have mercy, Jesu!--Soft, I did but dream.
O coward conscience, how dost thou afflict me!
The lights burn blue; it is now dead midnight.
Cold fearful drops stand on my trembling flesh.
What do I fear? Myself? There's none else by.
Richard loves Richard, that is, I am I.
Is there a murderer here? No. Yes, I am.
Then fly! What, from myself? Great reason why:
Lest I revenge. What, myself upon myself?
Alack, I love myself. Wherefore? For any good
That I myself have done unto myself?
O, no. Alas, I rather hate myself
For hateful deeds committed by myself.
I am a villain. Yet I lie; I am not.
Fool, of thyself speak well. Fool, do not flatter.
My conscience hath a thousand several tongues,
And every tongue brings in a several tale,
And every tale condemns me for a villain.
Perjury, perjury, in the highest degree;
Murder, stern murder, in the direst degree;
All several sins, all used in each degree,
Throng to the bar, crying all "Guilty, guilty!"
I shall despair. There is no creature loves me,
And if I die no soul will pity me.
And wherefore should they, since that I myself
Find in myself no pity to myself?
Methought the souls of all that I had murdered
Came to my tent, and every one did threat
Tomorrow's vengeance on the head of Richard.

lines: Zounds, who is there?
fullText: Zounds, who is there?

lines: O Ratcliffe, I have dreamed a fearful dream!
What think'st thou, will our friends prove all true?
fullText: O Ratcliffe, I have dreamed a fearful dream! What think'st thou, will our friends prove all true?

lines: O Ratcliffe, I fear, I fear.
fullText: O Ratcliffe, I fear, I fear.

lines: By the apostle Paul, shadows tonight
Have struck more terror to the soul of Richard
Than can the substance of ten thousand soldiers
Armed in proof and led by shallow Richmond.
'Tis not yet near day. Come, go with me.
Under our tents I'll play the eavesdropper
To see if any mean to shrink from me.

'Tis not yet near day. Come, go with me. Under our tents I'll play the eavesdropper To see if any mean to shrink from me.

lines: What said Northumberland as touching Richmond?
fullText: What said Northumberland as touching Richmond?

lines: He said the truth. And what said Surrey then?
fullText: He said the truth. And what said Surrey then?

lines: He was in the right, and so indeed it is.
Tell the clock there. Give me a calendar.
Who saw the sun today?
fullText: He was in the right, and so indeed it is. Tell the clock there. Give me a calendar. Who saw the sun today?

lines: Then he disdains to shine, for by the book
He should have braved the east an hour ago.
A black day will it be to somebody.
Ratcliffe!
fullText: Then he disdains to shine, for by the book He should have braved the east an hour ago. A black day will it be to somebody. Ratcliffe!

lines: The sun will not be seen today.
The sky doth frown and lour upon our army.
I would these dewy tears were from the ground.
Not shine today? Why, what is that to me
More than to Richmond, for the selfsame heaven
That frowns on me looks sadly upon him.
fullText: The sun will not be seen today. The sky doth frown and lour upon our army. I would these dewy tears were from the ground. Not shine today? Why, what is that to me More than to Richmond, for the selfsame heaven That frowns on me looks sadly upon him.

lines: Come, bustle, bustle. Caparison my horse.--
Call up Lord Stanley; bid him bring his power.--
I will lead forth my soldiers to the plain,
And thus my battle shall be ordered:
My foreward shall be drawn out all in length,
Consisting equally of horse and foot;
Our archers shall be placed in the midst.
John Duke of Norfolk, Thomas Earl of Surrey,
Shall have the leading of this foot and horse.
They thus directed, we will follow
In the main battle, whose puissance on either side
Shall be well winged with our chiefest horse.
This, and Saint George to boot!--What think'st
thou, Norfolk?

lines: Off with his son George's head!
fullText: Off with his son George's head!

lines: A thousand hearts are great within my bosom.
Advance our standards. Set upon our foes.
Our ancient word of courage, fair Saint George,
Inspire us with the spleen of fiery dragons.
Upon them! Victory sits on our helms.

lines: A horse, a horse, my kingdom for a horse!
fullText: A horse, a horse, my kingdom for a horse!

lines: Slave, I have set my life upon a cast,
And I will stand the hazard of the die.
I think there be six Richmonds in the field;
Five have I slain today instead of him.
A horse, a horse, my kingdom for a horse!
